coredata.dc:title: Amplified and selective detection of Ag+ ions based on electrically contacted enzymes on duplex-like DNA scaffolds
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Gang
Wang, Guangfeng
Zhu, Yanhong
Chen, Ling
He, Xiuping
Wang, Lun
Zhang, Xiaojun
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, ultrasensitive detection of Ag+ is demonstrated by a biocatalytic signal amplification system which is realized by only one DNA sequence based electrical contacted enzyme structure and the Au nanoparticles/Carbon nanodots (AuNPs/C-dots) composite immobilization platform. In the presence of Ag+, with the interaction of cytosine–Ag+–cytosine (C–Ag+–C), cytosine-rich DNA sequence labeled with methylene blue (MB) molecules near 5′ end and Glucose Oxidase (GOx) at 3′ end, has a self-hybridization and then forms a duplex-like structure which makes MB and GOx approach the AuNPs/C-dots modified electrode. MB units can then act as a relay that electrically contacts GOx with the AuNPs/C-dots modified electrode and activate the bioelectrocatalyzed oxidation of glucose to glucose acid. In consequence, based on the bioelectrocatalyzed signal amplification on the AuNPs/C-dots platform, Ag+ could be quantitatively detected in the range of 10−11–10−5 M with a low detection limit of 3pM. Also, there is an excellent selectivity against other interferential metal ions. The detection of Ag+ ions was realized by Ag+ self-induced conformational change of DNA scaffold which involved only one oligonucleotide showing its convenience and availability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrical contacted enzyme
Carbon nanodots
Cytosine
Ag+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the AuNPs/C-dots modified electrode
Preparation of the GOx modified DNA sequence
Assembly of the GOx–MB–S1 on the electrode
Electrochemical detection of glucose and Ag+
Characterization of the assembly on the electrode
CV responses of the duplex-like DNA system by electrically contacted enzyme toward glucose
CV responses toward different concentrations of glucose
Optimization of the variables of experimental conditions
The stability, selectivity and reproducibility of the sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of C-dots (A), AuNPs (B) and AuNPs/C-dots (C).

(A) Nyquist plot for electrochemical impedance measurements of different modified electrodes: bare GCE (a); C-dots/GCE (b); AuNPs/C-dots/GCE (c); GOx–MB–S1/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE (d) and GOx–MB–S1–Ag+-duplex/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE (e) in 5mM K3Fe(CN)6 with 0.1M KCl solution; (B and C) Real time binding QCM curves monitored of bare GE (B) and C-rich S1/GE (C) upon addition of C-rich DNA (B) or Ag+ ions (C). The red arrows indicate the time points when the sample touches the sensing area.

CV at the scan rate 50mV/s in PBS (pH 7.4) in the absence (red line) and presence of 40μM glucose (black line) of different electrodes under oxygen-free phenomenon: GOx–MB–S1–Ag+–duplex/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE (A); MB-S1-Ag+-duplex/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE (B); GOx–S1–Ag+–duplex/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE (C); GOx–MB–S1/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE (D) and GOx–MB–S1–Ag+-duplex/AuNPs/GCE (E). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) CV of GOx–MB–S1–Ag+–duplex/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE at the scan rate 50mV/s in PBS (pH 7.4) under oxygen-free phenomenon with 1μM Ag+ toward various concentrations of glucose (a–h): 0; 10; 20; 40; 60; 80; 100; 120μM; (B) The corresponding calibration curve of current vs. the concentration of glucose.

(A) CV at the scan rate 50mV/s and steady state amperometric current at 0.4V (Inset figure) corresponding to the bioelectrocatalytic oxidation of 100μM glucose under oxygen-free phenomenon using GOx–MB–S1–Ag+–duplex/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE incubated with different concentrations of Ag+: (a–g) 0.01; 0.1; 1; 10; 100; 1000; 10000nM. (B) The corresponding calibration curve of current vs. the concentration of Ag+. Inset: The corresponding calibration curve currents vs. the logarithmic concentration of Ag+. (C) Selectivity and (D) Interference study in the analysis of Ag+ by the duplex-like DNA system.
The fabrication and the electron transfer processes of duplex-like DNA structure.

head.ce:title: Amplified and selective detection of Ag+ ions based on electrically contacted enzymes on duplex-like DNA scaffolds

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chem–Biosensing, Anhui province; Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Anhui province; College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Center for Nano Science and Technology, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu 241000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chem–Biosensing, Anhui province; Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Anhui province; College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, ultrasensitive detection of Ag+ is demonstrated by a biocatalytic signal amplification system which is realized by only one DNA sequence based electrical contacted enzyme structure and the Au nanoparticles/Carbon nanodots (AuNPs/C-dots) composite immobilization platform. In the presence of Ag+, with the interaction of cytosine–Ag+–cytosine (C–Ag+–C), cytosine-rich DNA sequence labeled with methylene blue (MB) molecules near 5′ end and Glucose Oxidase (GOx) at 3′ end, has a self-hybridization and then forms a duplex-like structure which makes MB and GOx approach the AuNPs/C-dots modified electrode. MB units can then act as a relay that electrically contacts GOx with the AuNPs/C-dots modified electrode and activate the bioelectrocatalyzed oxidation of glucose to glucose acid. In consequence, based on the bioelectrocatalyzed signal amplification on the AuNPs/C-dots platform, Ag+ could be quantitatively detected in the range of 10−11–10−5 M with a low detection limit of 3pM. Also, there is an excellent selectivity against other interferential metal ions. The detection of Ag+ ions was realized by Ag+ self-induced conformational change of DNA scaffold which involved only one oligonucleotide showing its convenience and availability.

ce:section.ce:para: Silver ions (Ag+), surpassed only by mercury are recognized as one of the most hazardous metal pollutants due to their high potential toxicity to human health (Li et al., 2010; Sun et al., 2011; Lin et al., 2010) which may accumulate in the human body through the food chain or directly drinking, causing various serious problems such as cytotoxicity, organ failure, and reduction in mitochondrial function (Ryu et al., 2009). Thus, it is an important goal to develop methods for sensitive detection of Ag+ in aqueous solution. Currently, numerous conventional methods have been used for the determination of Ag+ ions including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) (Wu et al., 2008), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Li et al., 2007) and fluorescent or electrochemical chemosensing (Zhang et al., 2008; Taki et al., 2008). Among these methods, electrochemical methods offer good sensitivity and excellent selectivity (He et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2013). In addition, sophisticated instruments and complicated organic synthesis for special reagents is not needed. Therefore, it is very much required to develop an electrochemical method for Ag+ ions detection.

It is widely reported that the electrical contact of redox enzymes with electrodes is one of the most fundamental processes required for the development of bioelectronic devices, such as amperometric biosensors (Heller, 1996; Zayats et al., 2008), biofuel cells (Barton et al., 2004; Heller, 2004; Willner et al., 2009), etc. However, redox enzymes in bioelectronic devices usually lack direct electrical contact with electrodes because of the spatial separation of their redox centers from the conductive surfaces by the protein shells (Degani and Heller, 1988). So different methods to assemble enzyme on electrodes exhibiting electrical contact between the redox protein and electrode are discussed, including the assembly of enzyme–nanoparticle (Yehezkeli et al., 2011; Xiao et al., 2003), enzyme–carbon nanotube hybrids (Patolsky et al., 2004) and enzyme–DNA composite (Piperberg et al., 2009; Mor-Piperberg et al., 2010) on electrodes. Among these assembling methods, DNA monolayers are good scaffold for the reorganization of relay (cofactor) and enzyme units because it could lead to effective and controllable electrical contact of the enzymes with the electrode (Piperberg et al., 2009; Fruk et al., 2007; Wang et al., 2012a, 2012b).

Since Ag+ ions were found to specifically interact with the cytosine–cytosine (C–C) mismatch in DNA duplexes (Ono et al., 2008), it provides a novel rationale for Ag+ detection (Li et al., 2009; Oh and Lee, 2010; Zhao et al., 2010). Various C–Ag+–C based sensors have been developed with high sensitivity, convenience and good selectivity (Lin and Tseng, 2009; Li and Qi, 2011). Thus, inspired by the necessity of Ag+ detection, the advantage of DNA scaffold for electrical contact of enzyme and the excellent property of C–Ag+–C based sensor, can we study a C–Ag+–C scaffold based DNA sensor for Ag+ detection by the amplified signal generated from electrical contact of enzymes?

However, an effective immobilization platform for the DNA scaffold is also key in the detection system. With an explosion of interest in nanomaterial over the past decades (Rosi and Mirkin, 2005; Aragay et al., 2011), carbon nanodots (C-dots), first reported in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004), have subsequently attracted considerable attention (Cao et al., 2007; Yang et al., 2009). C-dots have a variety of applications, especially in the field of photocatalyst and electrocatalytic activities (Cao et al., 2007; Ray et al., 2009; Liu et al., 2009). The electronic properties of C-dots are suggestive of their potential ability as electrodes modifier to mediate electron transfer reactions with electroactive species in solution (Zhao et al., 2011; Cao et al., 2011; Long et al., 2012). In addition, besides C-dots exhibiting a high capability to promote some types of electron transfer reactions, it can also enhance the immobilization of molecules such as enzymes or antibodies on their surface (Li et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2011). Also, Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been extensively employed as immobilization platform because of their easy preparation, good biocompatibility, excellent catalytic effect and low toxicity (Lykakis et al., 2011; Fang et al., 2011; Cheng et al., 2003). What is more, it is very easy to immobilize thiolated DNA by the covalent bond of Au-S (Wang et al., 2012a, 2012b). In summary, the combination of C-dots with AuNPs (AuNPs/C-dots) as an immobilization platform will be fascinating and desirable.

Herein, a new DNA scaffold for the ultrasensitive detection of Ag+ is demonstrated by a novel biocatalytic signal amplification system of the electrical contacted enzyme. With the help of methylene blue (MB) as electron mediator, the electrical communication between the glucose oxidase (GOx) and the electrode is realized by the conformational change of the DNA scaffold on the AuNPs/C-dots immobilization platform. Because C–Ag+–C interaction is key for the formation of the DNA scaffold as designed, the biocatalytic response is relative with the amount of Ag+ which becomes the base of detecting Ag+ ions. In the present system, the bioelectrocatalytic signal of electrical contact of enzyme and nanocomposites platform is effective for the signal amplification.

HPLC-purified oligonucleotides (sequences are listed, S1: 5′–SH-(CH2)6-ACTATGTTCCTCCTCCTTTTCCACCACCAA-Biotin-3′, MB–S1: 5′–SH-(CH2)6-ACT-(MB)-ATGTTCCTCCTCCTTTTCCACCACCAA-Biotin-3′ was obtained from Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). GOx modified with avidin was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Gold (III) chloride trihydrate (99.9%), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and other reagents were obtained from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) without further purification. Ultrapure water (PSDK2-10-C) was used throughout all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the AuNPs/C-dots modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: In order to fabricate an effective immobilization platform, the AuNPs/C-dots modified glassy carbon electrode (AuNPs/C-dots/GCE) was prepared. Firstly, C-dots/GCE should be prepared. C-dots with low toxicity and high conductivity (Ray et al., 2009) were prepared according to the literature (Mao et al., 2010). Briefly, 0.5g of lampblack washed with acetone and dried at 110°C was added into 150mL of HNO3 (5mol/L), and the mixture was refluxed at 140°C for 12h. Then, the black solution was neutralized with sodium carbonate, loaded into dialysis bags (cutoff molecular weight 8000–14,000) for dialysis against ultrapure water for two days to purify the carbon dots. Acetone was added in the purified solution and then the bare C-dots were collected by centrifugation at 16,000rpm for 15min. The precipitation was dried under nitrogen flow and dispersed in ultrapure water. At last, the obtained bare C-dots solution was kept at 4°C until use. For modification of the electrode, 10μL of 1mg/mL homogeneous C-dots solution after ultrasonication was dropped onto the electrochemical cleaned GCE (Wang et al., 2012a, 2012b) and allowed to dry in ambient air for 24h, and then rinsed with distilled water to get the C-dots/GCE. Then, the C-dots/GCE were immersed in 0.1M KNO3 solution containing 0.2g/L HAuCl4 and then conducted for 60s at −0.26V (vs. SCE) with electrochemical working station for the electrochemical deposition of AuNPs. After that the AuNPs/C-dots/GCE was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the GOx modified DNA sequence
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of the MB and GOx dully labeled sequence, MB–S1 with the MB labeled at the base T near the 5′ end and the biotin labeled at 3′ end was designed. The introduction of MB in oligonucleotides S1 was completed by Sangon. We needed to further tether GOx at 3′ end by the interaction of biotin–streptavidin (Wang et al., 2012a, 2012b). Briefly, 100μL GOx-avidin (30μM) was reacted with the 3′-biotin-moiety of the S1 (30μM) for 2h in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.4) at room temperature, followed by the separation of dialysis to obtain GOx-MB dully labeled sequence (GOx–MB–S1). For the control assays, the introduction of GOx in oligonucleotides S1 without MB label (GOx–S1) was obtained under the same processes except for replacing MB–S1 with S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of the GOx–MB–S1 on the electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The immobilization of the GOx–MB–S1 was performed following the dropping method. The AuNPs/C-dots/GCE was immersed into 30μL Tris–HCl buffer (10mM, pH 7.4) containing 1.0μM GOx–MB–S1 probe and 1.0M NaCl in humidified chamber. The self-assembly through the thiol group of S1 and Au atoms was proceeded for 2h. Then the electrode was incubated in 1mM PBS (pH 7.4) with 5% BSA for 1h to eliminate the nonspecific-bonded DNA. After being thoroughly rinsed with the washing buffer (10mM PBS, pH 7.4, 100mM NaCl), GOx–MB–S1/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE was completed and ready for further use. The sensor obtained was found to maintain the activity for more than one week if stored at 4°C in the PBS (pH 7.4) that included 50mM NaCl. Under the same reaction condition, GOx–S1 modified electrode (GOx–S1/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE), MB–S1 without GOx modified electrode (MB–S1/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE) and GOx–MB-S1 modified on AuNPs/GCE (GOx–MB–S1/AuNPs/GCE) were constructed for control experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of glucose and Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: To form an electrical contacted enzyme biosensor for Ag+, we immersed GOx–MB–S1/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE in the PBS (pH 7.4) containing 1μM Ag+ ions and 10mM NaCl at 32°C for 50min. After the self-hybridization of the GOx–MB–S1 in the presence of Ag+, the electrical contacted enzyme biosensor was obtained which we name as GOx–MB–S1–Ag+–duplex/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE. For the control experiment, MB-S1/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE, GOx–S1/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE and GOx–MB–S1/AuNPs/GCE were incubated with 1μM Ag+ solution under the same conditions of the above for self-hybridization and forming MB–S1–Ag+-duplex/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE, GOx–S1–Ag+–duplex/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE, and GOx–MB–S1–Ag+–duplex/AuNPs/GCE, respectively.

For the study of bioelectrocatalyzed oxidation toward glucose, cyclic voltammetry (CV) of GOx–MB–S1–Ag+-duplex/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE or control electrodes (GOx–MB–S1–Ag+–duplex/AuNPs/GCE, GOx–MB–S1–Ag+–duplex/GCE) in PBS (pH 7.4) with different concentrations of glucose was performed. For ultrasensitive detection of Ag+, CVs of the biosensor in PBS (pH 7.4) corresponding to the bioelectrocatalytic oxidation of 100μM glucose using the GOx–MB–S1–Ag+–duplex/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE incubated with different concentrations of Ag+ were also carried out.

All the electrochemical measurements were performed on a model CHI660B electrochemical analyzer (ChenHua Instruments Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) controlled by a personal computer with a GCE or modified GCE as working electrode, a platinum wire as auxiliary electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference electrode. All the electrochemical experiments were carried out at room temperature though not illustrated. CV was performed at dissolved oxygen-free phenomenon in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.4) with or without glucose in the potential range from −0.4 to 0.4V using a step potential 0.01V at the scan rate 50mV/s. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) was performed in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/[Fe(CN)6]4− and 0.1M KCl in the frequency range from 0.1Hz to 100kHz with 5mV as the amplitude at a polarization potential of −0.18V. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the prepared composites were obtained using Hitachi S-4800 SEM (operated at 10kV). Centrifugation was performed using a HERMLEZ 36 HK apparatus (Wehingen, Germany).

Scheme 1 outlines the preparation processes of the bioelectrocatalytic activation of GOx on the duplex-like DNA scaffold and the generation of the electrocatalytic signal. The single strand oligonucleotide S1 acting as an anchor substrate on the composite AuNPs/C-dots/GCE platform with Au–S covalent bond is ready for the reconstitution of the relay-enzyme system. S1 includes 4 complementary A–T base pairs and 6 mismatched C bases if folded. However, the 4 complementary A–T base pairs are not sufficient to build a stable duplex structure. Once in the presence of Ag+, 6 C–Ag+–C base pairs will be formed and then cooperate to stabilize the duplex in which case a duplex-like DNA scaffold is constructed. Then GOx molecule labeled at 3′ end of S1 is brought to approach the surface of the electrode meeting the MB molecules as Scheme 1 shows. In the resulting duplex-like structure, MB unit acts as a relay that electrically contacts GOx with the electrode and activates the bioelectrocatalyzed oxidation of the glucose. The concentration of Ag+ ions added is directly relative to the extent of the biocatalyst associated with the electrode, so a novel Ag+ detection method is made by this efficient biocatalytic process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the assembly on the electrode
ce:section.ce:para: We used AuNPs/C-dots film as the immobilization platform for DNA scaffold in the present experiment. The characterization is shown in Fig. 1 with the typical SEM images. Fig. 1A shows the SEM image of C-dots/GCE. As we can see, the small blurry globules in Fig. 1A indicate that C-dots have been prepared successfully. However, we can only get the unclear image because of such a small size of C-dots. SEM image of the control AuNPs/GCE is shown in Fig. 1B and it׳s clear that bright balls are the gold nanoparticles. Fig. 1C is SEM image of AuNPs/C-dots/GCE and we can conclude that the homogeneous dispersion of AuNPs adheres to the surface of C-dots layer by comparing with Fig. 1A and B. The illustrious AuNPs can bind more S1 on the efficient AuNPs/C-dots immobilization platform.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) would provide detailed information about the varied property of surface-modified electrodes for each modification process (Bard and Faulkner, 1980). The typical impedance spectrum includes a semicircular portion whose diameter is equal to the electron-transfer resistance, Ret (Armstrong and Electroanal, 1986), which reflects the electron transfer kinetics of the redox probe at the electrode surface at higher frequencies (Bard and Faulkner, 1980). As Fig. 2A shows, EIS of a Nyquist plot was obtained in the assembling processes. We could observe that the bare GCE has a little semi-circular domain (curve a) about 400Ω. After the modification of C-dots and AuNPs, a visible decrease of Ret to almost straight lines (curve b, c) is obtained that implies a very low charge transfer resistance value of the AuNPs/C-dots/GCE. The reason may be that composite AuNPs/C-dots are an ideal conductive layer which can facilitate electron shuttling between the electrochemical probe and the electrode surface. Then, self-assembly of strand oligonucleotide GOx–MB–S1 on electrode exhibits much larger impedance (curve d) than before. The electron transfer becomes difficult because of the modification of GOx–MB–S1 with negative charge enhancement. Then the self-hybridization of GOx–MB–S1 in the presence of Ag+ results in a great decrease of Ret (curve e). This interesting phenomenon may be ascribed to easier electron transfer between MB and GOx when approaching, which can accelerate the electron transfer of the whole system. The result also coincides with the presumptive mechanism about the silver-induced conformational change of GOx–MB–S1 to duplex-like structure.

We also study the quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) assays to subsidiarily demonstrate the self-assembly of GOx–MB–S1 on the electrode and the adsorbing process of Ag+. The detailed QCM information is shown in Fig. 2B and C. After the addition of thiolated DNA sequence into the blank solution, frequency decreases quickly at the beginning and then slowly as the time increases. We deduce that the added DNA conjugated with the electrode by Au–S bond and results in the frequency response of QCM. Similarly, as Fig. 2B shows, frequency decreases quickly as the addition of Ag+ to the substrate solution at the beginning and then decreases slowly. We think the formation of mismatched C–Ag+–C pairs, resulting in the C–Ag+–C-duplex, increases the weight of substrate electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CV responses of the duplex-like DNA system by electrically contacted enzyme toward glucose
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the role of MB, GOx and Ag+ in the present electrically contacted enzymes system we studied CVs of different electrodes under oxygen-free phenomenon with (black line) and without (red line) the addition of glucose ( Fig. 3). As Fig. 3A shows, it is obvious that the bioelectrocatalytic oxidation currents of GOx–MB-S1–Ag+–duplex structure are generated only in the presence of glucose. To further validate the bioelectrocatalytic system, several control experiments are conducted as Fig. 3B–E shows. If there is no functionalized GOx on MB–S1, it does not lead to any bioelectrocatalytic oxidation of glucose (Fig. 3B), which implies that the assembly of GOx on the structure is an essential element to stimulate the bioelectrocatalytic oxidation of glucose. However, we are informed that both GOx–S1–Ag+–duplex/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE and GOx–MB–S1/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE have no obvious CV response towards glucose from Fig. 3C and D. The bioelectrocatalytic activity does not appear which implies that the MB units and Ag+-duplex DNA scaffold are also necessary to wire the enzyme׳s redox site and the electrode. According to the results and the literature (Xiao et al., 2003; Sekretaryova et al., 2012), we deduce that the MB unit acts as a relay that electrically contacts GOx with the electrode and activates the bioelectrocatalyzed oxidation of glucose.

In order to study the effect of the AuNPs/C-dots platform, the control experiment of AuNPs modified electrode alone is also done. As Fig. 3E shows, if we substitute the AuNPs/C-dots/GCE with the AuNPs/GCE to immobilize GOx–MB–S1 and repeat the same experimental processes, the CV responses toward the same concentrations of glucose become much weaker, which proves that the AuNPs/C-dots plays the role of signal amplification. It may result from the special property of C-dots which facilitates the immobilization of more S1 and meanwhile mediates electron transfer reactions. Finally, Table S1 is given a clear comparison to prove the necessity of each component in the electrical contact enzyme system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CV responses toward different concentrations of glucose
ce:section.ce:para: Further study on the response of GOx–MB–S1–Ag+–duplex/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE DNA system toward different concentrations of glucose is carried out based on the above result of CV under oxygen-free condition. As Fig. 4A shows, with the concentration of glucose increasing, the bioelectrocatalytic currents enlarge. The calibration curve corresponding to the CV responses of the modified electrode at different glucose concentrations is shown in Fig. 4B. It is found that the response current is linear with the glucose concentration in the range of 10–120μM and the detection limit is 3μM (S/N=3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the variables of experimental conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Parameters affecting the response of GOx–MB–S1–Ag+–duplex/AuNPs/C-dots/GCE toward glucose are investigated as follows. Optimization studies related to surface coverage of the GCE are in the Supplemental Information (SI) part of this manuscript (Fig. S1A). To find the proper pH value, we investigate the influence of pH on the performance of the GOx–MB–S1–Ag+–duplex DNA system over the pH range of 5.0–11.0 in Fig. S1B. Highly acidic or alkaline surrounding shows poor response as they would damage the immobilized GOx–MB–S1, especially in alkalinity. Finally an ideal pH of 7.4 is chosen for the experiments.

The effect of reaction temperature on the response of the system is also investigated. Fig. S1C depicts the peak current response of the sensor at varying reaction temperature ranging from 25 to 40°C. It is observed that the peak current increases with the temperature from 25 to 32°C and then decreases rapidly from 10.7 to 6.5μA as the reaction temperature increases from 32 to 40°C. Thus 32°C is chosen as the optimized reaction temperature.

The time-course of the Ag+ hybridized with C bases is studied by the electrochemical response signal to optimize the time so as to obtain the maximum loading of GOx on the sensor interface (Fig. S1D). The experimental data indicates that hybridization time has much more effect on the response signal, which implies that the adsorption quantity of Ag+ relies much on the time accretion. The oxidation peak current responses intensify with the hybridization time increasing and remain constant at a saturation value after about 45min, indicating that 50min as the incubation time is absolutely efficient. Accordingly, all subsequent analyses of Ag+ ions use 50min as the incubation time.

The response of the bioelectrocatalytic oxidation to glucose by the system must be relative to the concentration of Ag+ based on the discussion in the optimized time-course of the hybridized Ag+. Fig. 5A shows the CV under oxygen-free phenomenon of the present duplex-like DNA scaffold toward different concentrations of Ag+. As the concentration of Ag+ increases, the current response enlarges. The resulting calibration curve is depicted in Fig. 5B. We can observe that the current signals gradually increase with the concentrations of Ag+ in the pM level from 10−11 to 10−5 M. In consequence, a low detection limit of 3pM is evaluated using a signal three-fold of the background noise. We compare the result with some published Ag+ sensors and the detailed information about the linear ranger and detection limit is shown in Table S2. We can ascribe this enhanced sensitivity to the amplification path provided by the good biocatalytic system. Meanwhile, the present Ag+ sensor involves only one oligonucleotide DNA with no hybridization of two or more single strand oligonucleotide probes avoiding disadvantages such as hard operation and hybridization disorder. The whole detection is very fast with a detection time of 5min. and except the cost of the DNA, the whole sensor system does not have other expensive material or complicated organic synthesis showing its convenience and availability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability, selectivity and reproducibility of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To study the stability of our sensor, we investigated the CV responses of the sensor as a function of time, and we can get the information that the CV current still remains about 90% in 24h which can buttress the stability of our sensor (Fig. S2). Methods related to the sensing of metal ions by the enzyme–DNA structure concerns the specificity of the system (Wang et al., 2014). Thus selectivity of this detection method is tested by substituting the Ag+ in the system with various metal ions which are commonly present in real samples, such as K+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cs+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+ and Cd2+. As shown in Fig. 5C, each competing metal ion is tested at 3, 5, 10μM under the same experimental conditions. None of the tested metal ions gives peak currents higher than half of that produced by 30, 50, 100nM Ag+ ions. Such excellent selectivity is attributed to the specific C–Ag+–C base pairing which relates closely with the extent for the production of enzymatic signal. Moreover, as an excellent biosensor, it should not be disturbed by other coexisting ions. In this case, an interference study was performed. As shown in Fig. 5D, it further displays an excellent anti-interference capability in the presence of other coexisting ions. For the reproducibility of our sensor, it was evaluated by using the coefficient of variation of intra- and inter-assays. Taking 100, 500 and 1000nM Ag+, for example, the intra-assay and inter-assay of the sensor was evaluated using one sensor for five repeat assays and five sensors for one time assay, respectively. The RSDs of the intra-assay and inter-assay were 4.4%, 5.1%, 4.7% and 5.2%, 4.9%, 3.7% for 100, 500 and 1000nM Ag+, respectively. Thus, the reproducibility of our sensor is satisfactory.

Five river water samples were collected respectively at five sewage outfalls along the Qingyi River, and then were filtered through a 0.2mM membrane to remove oils and other organic impurities. For the recovery test, the spiked water samples were diluted with equal volume of PBS. Subsequently, the river water samples were spiked with standard solutions of Ag+ over the concentrations from 0.01 to 500nM prior to measurement. All the results were measured in PBS solution containing 100μM glucose. The detailed result of the detection is shown in Table S3 in the SI part.

In this paper, we have demonstrated a novel method for Ag+ detection with electrically contacted enzyme structure on a novel AuNPs/C-dots platform. Because of the enzymatic signal amplification on the innovative platform and excellent selectivity resulting from the merits of high specificity of the C-Ag+-C interaction, the sensitivity and selectivity of the present method to silver ion is excellent. Meanwhile the glucose can be detected by the electrical contacted enzyme system induced by DNA scaffold which involved only one oligonucleotide showing its convenience and availability. The introduction of carbon nanodots in the immobilization platform improved the signal and developed its application in DNA based electrochemical sensor. Considering the good performance of this enzyme-amplified electrochemical Ag+ sensor, it is expected to obtain great successes in environmental monitoring. Furthermore, alternative sensing devices may be envisaged as well since other natural or synthetic bases selectively can bind other metal ions (e.g., Hg2+ by thymine).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the projects (21073001, 21005001 and 21371007) from National Natural Science Foundation of China, Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation (1208085QB28), Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Youth (1408085J03), Natural Science Foundation of Anhui (KJ2012A139) and the Program for Innovative Research Team at Anhui Normal University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2014.03.017.
